keyLegislation: A New Tax System (Goods and Services Tax) Act 1999 (GST Act)
Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorism Financing Act 2006 (AML/CTF Act)
keyConsiderations: A sole trader is not a separate legal entity — the individual and the business are the same legal person, meaning personal liability for all business debts and obligations
Bitcoin held as a business asset is treated differently for tax purposes than Bitcoin held as a personal investment
If Bitcoin is held as a business asset for the purpose of trade or exchange (carrying on a business of Bitcoin trading), profits are assessed as ordinary income, not capital gains
If Bitcoin is held as a treasury reserve or long-term investment by the business, CGT rules apply on disposal
The distinction between trading stock and CGT asset depends on the taxpayer's purpose, frequency of transactions, and overall conduct
GST does not apply to sales of digital currency from 1 July 2017 under the GST Act amendments
Sole traders must include Bitcoin gains in their personal income tax return and pay tax at their marginal rate
The CGT discount of 50% is available for Bitcoin held for more than 12 months (as a CGT asset, not trading stock)
Small business CGT concessions (Division 152 ITAA 1997) may apply if the sole trader's aggregated turnover is under $2 million and net asset value is under $6 million
Business Activity Statement (BAS) reporting obligations continue as normal — Bitcoin transactions do not attract GST but must be considered in BAS preparation
Record keeping requirements are the same as for any business income and expenses — 5 year retention period
commonRisks: Confusion between personal and business Bitcoin holdings leading to incorrect tax treatment
Failure to maintain adequate records of acquisition costs, leading to inability to calculate accurate capital gains
Being classified as a Bitcoin trader by the ATO when the intention was to hold as an investment (or vice versa), resulting in unexpected tax consequences
Loss of Bitcoin through poor security practices with no corporate veil or insurance protection
Personal liability exposure — as a sole trader, all business debts and liabilities (including losses from Bitcoin holdings) are personal liabilities
Insufficient cash reserves to pay tax on unrealised or realised gains from Bitcoin appreciation
Failure to report Bitcoin disposals including when using Bitcoin to pay for goods or services
Not accounting for Bitcoin received as payment for goods or services as assessable income
Inadequate separation between personal and business finances making it difficult to track the business purpose of Bitcoin holdings
Exchange or custody failure with no recourse beyond personal claims
recommendedProfessionals: Registered Tax Agent with cryptocurrency experience
Accountant familiar with small business CGT concessions
IT Security Consultant (for custody and key management)
